claim: The density ρ of Fe-Mn-C-Al austenite steel follows ρ_Fe-Mn-C-Al = 8.15 - 0.101(wt% Al) - 0.41(wt% C) - 0.0085(wt% Mn).

C addition plays the most obvious density reduction effect, followed by Al and Mn, but the most significant alloying effect on density reduction is contributed from Al addition because the density of Al (2.7 g cm⁻³) is much lighter than that of Mn (7.2 g cm⁻³).
evidence_text: which shows that C addition plays the most obvious density reduction effect, and then Al and Mn alloying follow. … the most significant alloying effect on density reduction of Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steel shall be contributed from Al addition.

claim: The lattice constant α of Fe-Mn-C-Al austenite follows α_Fe-Mn-C-Al = 0.35721 + 0.0002771(wt% Al) + 0.0044089(wt% C) + 0.0001166(wt% Mn) in nm.
Yield strength σ_γ (MPa) of Fe-Mn-C-Al austenite is related to solute content by σ_γ = 10.5 Al_wt% + 351.5; σ_γ = 0.58 Mn_wt% + 328.0; σ_γ = 180.7 C_wt% + 250.7.
In annealed fully austenite low-density steel, abundant annealing twins are observed and the grains are fully recovered, as illustrated in Figure 1c.
evidence_text: The microstructure observation shows that abundant annealing twins are observed in austenite low-density steel, and the grains are fully recovered. … the illustrations are presented in Figure 1c

claim: In austenite-based duplex low-density steel, elongated ferrite phase grains along the rolling direction are observed, as shown in Figure 1d.
evidence_text: elongated ferrite phase grains along the rolling direction are shown in the austenite-based duplex steel. … the illustrations are presented in Figure 1d

claim: The 900 °C isothermal section is critical for low-density steel alloying design because it is close to hot rolling temperature; Figure 2 shows the isothermal sections of Fe–(10, 20, 30 wt%)Mn–xC–yAl alloys at 900 °C based on experiments and CALPHAD.
evidence_text: The isothermal sections of Fe–(10, 20, 30 wt%)Mn–xC–yAl alloys at 900 °C, based on experiments and CALPHAD, are exhibited in Figure 2 … the 900 °C processing temperature is close to hot rolling temperature in the production line.

claim: With Mn content increasing, austenite region expands within high Al concentration area (>8 wt%), and κ phase region expands within high C (>3 wt%) and high Al (>10 wt%); with C content >1.2 wt%, M3C and κ phases emerge; with Al content increasing, ferrite region expands with high Al concentration in 20–30 wt% Mn containing steel.
evidence_text: 1) with Mn content increasing, austenite (γ) region expands within high Al concentration area (>8 wt%), and the κ phase region expands within high C (>3 wt%) and high Al concentrations area (>10 wt%); 2) with C content increasing to be higher than 1.2 wt%, M3C and κ phases emerge …; and 3) with Al content increasing, ferrite (α) phase region expands with high Al concentration in the range of 20–30 wt% Mn containing Fe–Mn–C–Al steel.

claim: When fully austenite and austenite-based duplex Fe–Mn–C–Al steel is aged at 400–650 °C for 0.5–100 h, the austenite undergoes stepwise decomposition: γ → γ′ + γ′′ → γ′ + L1₂(SRO) → γ + κ.
evidence_text: When the full austenite and austenite-based duplex Fe–Mn–C–Al steel is aging treated at 400–650 °C with time of 0.5–100 h, the chance of modulation of C and Al appears within the austenite and then decomposition step by step as the following sequence γ → γ′ + γ′′ → γ′ + L1₂(SRO) → γ + κ

claim: In Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steel, Fe(Mn)3C carbide precipitates in low Al-containing steel (Al <7 wt%), while κ precipitation dominates in high Al-containing steel (Al ≥7 wt%); both precipitates form within austenite grains and along grain boundaries. The decomposition halts at L12(SRO) in low Al steels, whereas κ precipitation continues in high C and Al steels.
evidence_text: With sufficient aging or slow cooling treatments, the κ and Fe(Mn)3C precipitations form within austenite grains and along the grain boundary. It shall be pointed out that the Fe(Mn)3C carbide was commonly observed in low Al-containing steel (<7 wt%), while the κ precipitation occurs in high Alcontaining steel (≥7 wt%). This is because the decomposition process stops at L12(SRO) production in low Al-containing steel, while the κ precipitation continues to react in high C and Al-containing steel.

claim: In Fe–28Mn–8.5Al–1C–1.2Si low-density steel, κ phase precipitates from the austenite matrix when the cooling rate is slower than 4.4 °C h⁻¹, as shown in the γ phase decomposition diagram (Figure 3).
evidence_text: This plot clearly illustrates that the κ phase will decompose from the austenite matrix of Fe–28Mn–8.5Al–1C–1.2Si low-density steel, when the cooling rate is slower than −4.4 °C h⁻¹.

claim: TWIP, TRIP, and SRO strengthening mechanisms play significant roles in controlling the work hardening behavior of Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steel.
evidence_text: TWIP as well as TRIP strengthening mechanism, and the SRO strengthening effects play the significant roles in controlling work hardening behavior.

claim: The short-range ordering (SRO) effect is observed in Fe–Mn–C–Al steel with more than 6 wt% Al, and the parameter W in Fe–22Mn–(0,3,6)Al steels decreases with increasing Al content, indicating stronger dislocation planar slip, as shown in Figure 6a.
evidence_text: SRO effect is observed in high Al-containing Fe–Mn–C–Al steel with > 6 wt%. ... the parameter W of Fe–22Mn–(0, 3, and 6) Al (wt%) low-density steel decreases with Al content increasing, as shown in a. Figure 6

claim: TEM observation of narrow and dense coplanar slip bands (Figure 6b,c) demonstrates grain subdivision and dynamic Hall–Petch hardening effect in Fe–Mn–C–Al steel with SRO.
evidence_text: The observation of the narrow and dense coplanar slip bands under TEM observation and the SRO effect on work hardening rate evolution are displayed in Figure 6b,c, respectively.[81] The observation of the narrow and dense coplanar slip bands proves that the phenomena of the grain subdivision and dynamic Hall–Petch hardening effect work.

claim: Overaging at 625 °C for 192 h in Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steel causes κ-carbide particles to be sheared by planar dislocation bands, and the large size and high volume fraction of κ-carbide reduce work hardening capacity compared with short-time aging (<10 h).
evidence_text: with overaging treatment (625 °C) for 192 h, the κ-carbide particles are observed and sheared by the planar dislocation band. Subsequently, the large size and high-volume fraction of the κ-carbide particles will introduce limited chances of particles being sheared by planar dislocations, resulting in lower work hardening capacity compared with that of short time aging treated sample (aging time less than 10 h).

claim: Yield strength improvement Δσp from precipitates in Fe–Mn–C–Al steel follows: Δσp = 0.847·M·(1.2/π)·G·b·(3/(2π))^0.5·f^0.5/D·ln(D/(2b)), with Taylor factor M = 3.06 for fcc metals.
evidence_text: Δσp = 0.847 · M · (1.2/π) · G · b · sqrt(3/(2π)) · f^0.5/D · ln(D/(2b)) (8) where the M is Taylor factor (3.06 for face-centered cubic, fcc, metals)

claim: Figure 7 presents calculated Δσp values for various carbides: TiC (20 nm, 150 MPa), V(C,N) (30 nm, 200 MPa), V4C3 (80 nm, 400 MPa), V(C,N) (80 nm, 400 MPa), V(C,N) (100 nm, 300 MPa; 100 nm, 250 MPa), and NbC (100 nm, 100 MPa).

Figure 8a shows for Fe–22.33Mn–1.35C–9.87Al (wt%) steel that wear rate decreases with aging time at 500, 550, and 600 °C; e.g., at 600 °C, wear rate drops from 0.18 to 0.11 mm³/Nm for 1–3 h (linear fit: y = -0.0374x + 0.2146, R² = 0.99).

In Fe–22.33Mn–1.35C–9.87Al (wt%) steel, incoherent κ particles formed after higher aging temperature and longer time inhibit crack nucleation during wear, and the steel aged at 600 °C for 3 h exhibits good wear resistance (Figure 8b,c).
evidence_text: Sohrabizadeh et al. established a function of aging time and wear rate in Fe–22.33Mn–1.35C–9.87Al (wt%), ... This function pointed out the incoherent κ particles within the austenite matrix, after higher aging temperature and longer time, inhibited crack nucleation during the wear test. The detailed scanning electron microscope (SEM) observation of the worn surface and debris of the 600 °C aged steel is shown in Figure 8b,c. The observation result supports that 600 °C aged steel has good performance of wear resistance.

claim: In Fe–28Mn–1C–8.5Al (wt%) low-density steel, overaging at 550 °C for >2 h coarsens κ particles at grain boundaries and increases wear rate (Figure 8d); microcracks propagate along broken coarse κ particles, initiating delamination damage (Figure 8e).
evidence_text: Peng et al. indicated that the wear rate of Fe–28Mn–1C–8.5Al (wt%) low-density steel deteriorated after overaging treatment with longer than 2 h at temperature of 550 °C due to the coarsen κ particles around the grain boundaries, as shown in Figure 8d,e. With the detailed observation of the lamination region in the 550 °C annealed for 4 h sample, the propagation of microcracks is detected along the broken coarse κ particles, indicating that the overaged κ particles are the initiation of cracks, delamination, and other irreparable damages.

claim: Metal injection molding (MIM) of Fe–22Mn–0.4C–1.5Al–1.5Si (wt%) steel powder mixed with HDPE and paraffin wax, followed by sintering at 1200–1360 °C, results in a fully austenitic structure with uniformly distributed manganese oxide particles (Figure 9).
evidence_text: Garcia-Aguirre et al. combine the Fe–22Mn–0.4C–1.5Al–1.5Si (wt%) steel powder with highdensity polyethylene (HDPE) and paraffin wax (PW) for mixing process and immersed in the solvent. The sintering process of annealing temperature between 1200 °C and 1360 °C is employed to have fully austenitic structure with equally distributed manganese oxide particles as shown in Figure 9.

claim: Figure 6 illustrates the evolution of W_R with Al content to predict dislocation slipping transition from wavy to planar mode, ECCI micrographs of microbands, and tensile/work hardening curves of Fe–Mn–C–Al steel with SRO effect.
evidence_text: Figure 6. a) The evolution of W_R with Al content to predict dislocation slipping transition from (a1) wavy to (a2) planar mode, b) microstructure observation of microbands under ECCI, and c) tensile and work hardening curves of Fe–Mn–C–Al steel with SRO effect.

claim: Metal injection molding (MIM) of Fe–22Mn–0.4C–1.5Al–1.5Si steel powder with HDPE and paraffin wax, followed by sintering at 1200–1360 °C for 1–3 h, produces a fully austenitic structure with equally distributed manganese oxide particles (Figure 9).
evidence_text: Garcia-Aguirre et al.[91] combine the Fe–22Mn–0.4C–1.5Al–1.5Si (wt%) steel powder with highdensity polyethylene (HDPE) and paraffin wax (PW) for mixing process and immersed in the solvent. The sintering process of annealing temperature between 1200 °C and 1360 °C is employed to have fully austenitic structure with equally distributed manganese oxide particles as shown in Figure 9

claim: Cryogenic impact energy degradation is caused by secondary phase transformation: martensite forms in 17 wt% Mn steel and δ-ferrite forms in 8 wt% Al steel, leading to microcrack nucleation and propagation along phase boundaries.
evidence_text: the secondary phase transformation plays the critical role in deteriorating impact energy at cryogenic temperature. With Mn alloying content decreasing to 17 wt% in Fe–xMn–0.45C–2Al (wt%) steel, the martensite phase was detected during plastic deformation. In the meanwhile, the δ-ferrite formed in 8 wt% Al-containing Fe–18Mn–0.6C–xAl steel. The microcracks easily nucleate and propagate along the phase boundary

claim: Figure 10 illustrates that combining alloying design with annealing and aging treatments achieves low-density steel with lower density, higher strength, better wear resistance, and low material cost.
evidence_text: With desirable control of these two factors, the characteristics of lower density, higher strength, better wear resistance, and low material cost can be achieved for low-density steel development, as shown in Figure 10.

claim: Increasing Mn content increases MnO at the mold–shell interface, reducing heat transfer and lubrication stability, and promotes shrinkage volume fraction, causing voids and cracks.
evidence_text: With addition of Mn elements into steel will bring interfacial reaction problems, where the content of MnO significantly increases and then reduces the heat transfer from inside of ingot to outside, resulting in unstable heat transfer and lubrication between mold and solidifying shell. Besides, with Mn addition increasing, the shrinkage volume fraction promotes. This behavior will bring unavoidable voids and cracks left in Fe–Mn–C–Al steel ingots and rolled sheets

claim: Future work should focus on metallurgy to solve Mn- and Al-related impurity and shrinkage problems, optimize aging for strength-ductility balance, lower raw material costs, and develop hot stamping technology.
evidence_text: the future work will focus on the metallurgy improvement to solve the Mn- and Al-enriched problems, such as impurity, inclusions, shrinkage, and other challenges. Massive and stable production of Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steel is the critical way to promote the wide application... other crucial challenges need to be addressed, such as 1) aging treatment optimization to find the balance of strength and ductility with κ phase precipitation; 2) lowering raw material cost, especially reducing usage of Al and Mn as additions, while maintaining good mechanical properties; and 3) developing hot stamping technology
